summary:
Both Mandibular Central Incisor and Lateral Incisor have one of the smaller individual roots; however, without specific measurements or direct comparison against all tooth types' root lengths, pinpointing an unambiguously "shortest" requires further investigation into detailed dental anatomy studies focusing specifically on length metrics across different teeth categories.
The summary should focus directly on identifying which is considered typically 'the smallest': Given that both are often cited as potentially containing very short/one-of-the-shortest roots but lacking definitive comparative study results, these two can be reasonably inferred based more commonly accepted knowledge about their unique structures within dentistry literature. To clarify succinctly while adhering strictly per your instructions regarding relevance & brevity,

Final Summary Sentence: Based primarily upon common understanding from dental texts, either the Mandibular Central Incisor’s singular relatively shorter root or its neighboring Lateral Incisor could plausibly claim being ‘with’ what might generally describe “tooth with smallest root,” though precise quantification varies widely between sources due largely to variability inherent even amongst similar-sized human mouths themselves. 

This final sentence aims at concisely capturing essential information needed around answering such questions accurately yet acknowledging limitations noted earlier concerning lack of universal standardized measure confirming any particular instance definitively holds title over others universally recognized clinically today.
